episode_meta.title: Ole Reissmann: Revolutionizing a Legacy Newsroom with AI at Germany's Der Spiegel

ai_tools: Der Spiegel internal AI platform (custom-built tool using multiple LLMs)
Chrome browser plugin (context tool built by Klaus Hueck)

use_cases.description: Automated generation of SEO headlines for approximately 150 articles per day, freeing SEO editors to do higher-value work
use_cases.outcome: SEO editors freed up time from routine headline checking to do more useful work

use_cases.description: AI-assisted fact-checking tool that extracts factual statements from articles, then verifies them against LLM knowledge, internal article database, and web searches using AI agents visiting multiple websites
use_cases.outcome: Gets fact-checkers 20-40% of the way there, allowing the department to focus on more complex verification tasks. Provides a confidence score for each fact.

use_cases.description: Chrome browser plugin that lets users select a word or phrase in a Spiegel article, searches the Spiegel archive, and returns a summarized background paragraph with linked sources
use_cases.outcome: Being tested internally with newsroom staff for quality before potential wider rollout

use_cases.description: AI-generated article summaries tested on sister publication Manager Magazin, with human review and approval before publication
use_cases.outcome: Experiment running for several weeks; impact on audience usage data still being evaluated

use_cases.description: Text-to-speech conversion allowing users to listen to every article on the platform
use_cases.outcome: Running for approximately one and a half years; looking to upgrade to newer, more human-sounding models

use_cases.description: Automated podcast generation pipeline: ingests Spiegel articles on breaking news, generates audio-optimized scripts, converts to speech, and can translate into multiple languages (Russian, English, Turkish). Includes human review of scripts by native speakers before publication.
use_cases.outcome: Prototype/proof of concept built; to be shown to company but not yet in production

use_cases.description: Gender report that automatically extracts names from articles, identifies gender, and produces monthly departmental reports on gender representation in coverage
use_cases.tool_used: GPT (for name extraction and gender identification)
use_cases.outcome: Multi-year data collected showing significantly more men mentioned than women; used to inform editorial discussions about more equal reporting

use_cases.description: Building a vector database of Der Spiegel's article archive to enable personalization and serve as foundation for multiple AI-powered products
use_cases.outcome: Identified as the most important foundational project; under development

use_cases.description: Personalization of news content and user experience, including format preferences (read, listen, summary)
use_cases.outcome: Planning to start small and deliberately later in the year; technical foundations being built

use_cases.description: Internal AI platform providing secure access to multiple AI models (ChatGPT, Google, etc.) within data protection and data security guidelines, with data kept in Europe via Microsoft Azure
use_cases.tool_used: Microsoft Azure, ChatGPT Enterprise, Google models
use_cases.outcome: Built in-house; staff can use AI securely; another publishing house is collaborating on the software

use_cases.description: Basic AI training sessions covering ChatGPT and image generation tools (Midjourney, DALL-E) with access to a prompt library afterwards
use_cases.outcome: Over 870 people trained; sessions book out immediately; offering more sessions

use_cases.description: Collaborative press archive database with all articles published in Germany since the Federal Republic, exploring generative AI chat interface for semantic search
use_cases.organization: Der Spiegel and Axel Springer (joint venture)
use_cases.outcome: Prototype exists for chatting with the archive; currently offers semantic search and entity extraction

use_cases.description: Using Google NotebookLM to upload podcast transcripts from Newsroom Robots, creating a RAG system to query expert perspectives on an AI project proposal
use_cases.outcome: Successfully used insights to inform his application to the CUNY AI Journalism Lab

use_cases.description: Translations, transcriptions, and spell checking using traditional AI
use_cases.outcome: In use for years prior to generative AI wave

key_claims.claim: Der Spiegel's SEO department checks approximately 150 articles on any given day for good SEO headlines
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: More than 870 people at Der Spiegel have taken part in basic AI training sessions on ChatGPT and image generation tools
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: ChatGPT is not very good at fact-checking directly, but it can extract factual statements from sentences which can then be verified against other sources
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: The AI fact-checking tool only gets you 20-40% of the way there; the rest requires human expertise for complicated verification
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: On average, many more men than women appear in Der Spiegel's articles, as revealed by the AI-powered gender report
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: Der Spiegel has about 10 AI projects going on, with only one or two being external/audience-facing
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: Smart speakers from four years ago mostly failed because people only used them for music and weather; now LLM-powered voice interfaces may enable a second coming
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: AI model costs are getting cheaper and cheaper by the month, but user-facing products at scale with potentially millions of users remain expensive
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: Der Spiegel does not want to use photorealistic AI-generated images because they do not want to make up the truth or trick anybody
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

key_claims.claim: He uploaded his blood work to ChatGPT and received a comprehensive assessment that matched what his doctor told him
key_claims.speaker: Ole Reissmann

challenges.description: LLMs present themselves like search engines but produce hallucinations and factual errors, making them unreliable for direct fact retrieval
Maintaining audience trust while deploying AI, especially for a legacy brand with older paying subscribers who expect factual accuracy
Newer text-to-speech models are built for shorter content and speech assistants, causing difficulties with Der Spiegel's long-form articles (40+ minutes)
High compute costs and energy consumption for user-facing AI products at scale with potentially millions of users
AI companies trained on publisher content without adequate consent or compensation; new awareness around data protection needed
Software tools constantly add new AI features (e.g., image manipulation buttons) that may cross editorial lines, and the newsroom cannot control external software tools
Setting strict AI guidelines is difficult because the technology and user expectations are shifting constantly
Being a major publishing house but still a small to mid-sized company, making technology development hard and collaboration necessary
Prioritization across approximately 10 concurrent AI projects with limited resources in any given year
Gender identification by AI is not 100% accurate, and attempting to guess age of people mentioned in articles did not work well

quotes.text: Maybe the most obvious use cases of generative AI are not the ones we should go after. Because like first we have to ask ourselves why we would want to use AI and what AI is actually good at. And does it serve our mission and does it align with our values?
quotes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

quotes.text: We run the fact checking tool not to get rid of the fact checking department, no no, to the contrary. So we run the fact checking tool which is in beta right now and we will hopefully have an up and running version in the next couple of months so everybody can use it at the company. And it only gets you like I don't know twenty, thirty, forty percent there. And the other percent, that's like the stuff humans are really needed for and which is really complicated and where you can't do a web search, like where people have to really dig into it.
quotes.speaker: Ole Reissmann
quotes.topic: AI augmenting rather than replacing fact-checkers

quotes.text: We are really in the trust business and if we were to put something out, we want to make sure that not only a younger audience is happy, but our core audience, which tends to be a little bit older, our paying subscribers, we want to offer them factual information and we really want to make sure that we do not disappoint our readers and that we do not lose trust.
quotes.speaker: Ole Reissmann
quotes.topic: Trust and audience considerations in AI deployment

quotes.text: There's so much talk about, oh well, these AI LLMs, they are biased. And now it's actually an LLM that's helping us discover our biases. I think that's quite beautiful.
quotes.speaker: Ole Reissmann
quotes.topic: Using AI to audit editorial bias in gender representation

quotes.text: I do not trust the LLM and the probability model... I'm gonna put some authoritative sources in there and ask about what did the experts actually say. And I think it gives you a much more interesting, nuanced answer compared to the LLM looks into itself and spills out some hallucinations and maybe some generalizations.
quotes.speaker: Ole Reissmann
quotes.topic: RAG approach vs. relying on LLM knowledge alone

quotes.text: If we are thinking of user-facing products, I think that's when it gets really expensive, especially with the current models, even though they're getting cheaper and cheaper by the month. We really have to look into like if we were to deploy a user-facing product and potentially millions of people make use of these tools, that's for one costly. And then it's a question of, I mean, this compute, it doesn't come from nowhere, it costs energy, and is this a good use of energy right now.
quotes.speaker: Ole Reissmann
quotes.topic: Cost and sustainability concerns for AI at scale

recommendations.advice: Do not start with the most obvious use cases of generative AI; first ask whether AI serves your mission and aligns with your values
recommendations.speaker: Ole Reissmann

recommendations.advice: Give the LLM context and authoritative sources (RAG approach) rather than relying on its internal knowledge, which produces hallucinations and generalizations
recommendations.speaker: Ole Reissmann

recommendations.advice: Train as many people as possible on AI fundamentals so decisions about AI usage come from the whole organization, not just leadership
recommendations.speaker: Ole Reissmann

recommendations.advice: Start with principles rather than strict guidelines, since the technology and user expectations are constantly changing
recommendations.speaker: Ole Reissmann

recommendations.advice: Build foundational data infrastructure (vector databases) first before pursuing consumer-facing AI products, as it enables many downstream applications
recommendations.speaker: Ole Reissmann

recommendations.advice: Collaborate with other publishing houses on shared technology and resources, since even major publishers are effectively small to mid-sized tech companies
recommendations.speaker: Ole Reissmann

recommendations.advice: Do not rush to be first with AI-powered consumer products; it is better to avoid stupid mistakes than to be first to market, especially for trust-based brands
recommendations.speaker: Ole Reissmann

recommendations.advice: Consider the energy and compute cost sustainability of AI products, especially for user-facing tools at scale; use smaller, more efficient models where possible
recommendations.speaker: Ole Reissmann
